prerequisites.question: A spring is stretched a distance x from its natural length. Hooke's law says the spring pulls back with a force of magnitude:
options: F = k x, directed back toward equilibrium
F = k x², directed away from equilibrium
F = m g, straight down
F = k / x, toward equilibrium
prerequisites.answer: F = k x, directed back toward equilibrium
prerequisites.remediation: Hooke's law is F = -kx: the restoring force grows in proportion to how far you stretch or compress the spring, and always points back toward equilibrium.

prerequisites.question: A 'restoring force' is a force that always:
options: points back toward the equilibrium position
points in the direction of motion
grows without limit as the object speeds up
acts only at the moment of release
prerequisites.answer: points back toward the equilibrium position
prerequisites.remediation: A restoring force always pushes or pulls the object back toward its resting point. That single property is what makes motion oscillate rather than fly off.

prerequisites.question: As the angle θ increases from 0, the function cos(θ):
options: starts at its maximum of 1 and oscillates smoothly between +1 and −1
starts at 0 and only ever increases
stays constant at 1
jumps between +1 and −1 with nothing in between
prerequisites.answer: starts at its maximum of 1 and oscillates smoothly between +1 and −1
prerequisites.remediation: cos(θ) begins at 1 when θ = 0, falls to −1, and returns to 1 every 2π — a smooth repeating wiggle. That is exactly the shape traced by an object in SHM released from rest.

beats.title: The universe keeps a beat
beats.goal: Hook: oscillation is everywhere. Show a mass bobbing on a spring with its restoring force, and pose the driving question — how fast does it tick, and why does the same math draw waves?

skeleton.text: A heartbeat, a child on a swing, a plucked guitar string, an atom in a crystal — they all share one motion. Watch this mass on a spring.
Pull it aside and release: the red restoring force always yanks it back toward the middle, so it overshoots, returns, overshoots again — over and over.
That endless back-and-forth is Simple Harmonic Motion. Today we'll find exactly how fast it ticks — and why the very same math draws a wave.

beats.title: The spring's clock
beats.goal: Show x(t) = A·cos(√(k/m)·t): the period is set by stiffness k and mass m, never amplitude. Have the student predict the effect of mass, then verify with the springPeriod fact.

skeleton.text: The displacement follows a cosine: x(t) = A·cos(√(k/m)·t). A stiffer spring (bigger k) ticks faster; a heavier mass (bigger m) ticks slower.
With k = 20 N/m and m = 1 kg, one full cycle takes T = {{springPeriod(1,20)}} s — that's the number printed under the spring.
Predict before you touch a thing: if you DOUBLE the mass, does the period double too — or grow by less than that?
Now drag the mass to 2 kg. The period stretches only to T = {{springPeriod(2,20)}} s — about √2 ≈ 1.41 times longer, not 2, because T grows as the square root of m.

check.text: For a mass on a spring, which single change makes the period LONGER?
options: Increasing the mass
Increasing the stiffness k
Increasing the amplitude
Releasing it from a smaller displacement

beats.title: A different clock: the pendulum
beats.goal: Show the pendulum is also SHM for small angles, but its period depends on length and gravity — not mass. Contrast it sharply with the spring.

skeleton.title: Does a swing keep the same beat?

skeleton.text: A pendulum swings with the same rhythmic overshoot — for small angles it's SHM as well. But its clock is set by something completely different.
The pendulum's period is T = 2π√(L/g). With L = 2 m, one full swing takes T = {{pendPeriod(2)}} s — and notice there is no mass anywhere in that formula.
Predict, then test: to make the swing take twice as long, do you double the length — or quadruple it?

check.text: A pendulum clock keeps perfect time. You then hang a HEAVIER bob on the same string. Its period will:
options: stay the same — a pendulum's period does not depend on mass
get longer, because the bob is heavier
get shorter, because gravity pulls harder
drop to zero
check.answer: stay the same — a pendulum's period does not depend on mass

beats.title: The hidden sine wave
beats.goal: Reveal the wave connection: a row of SHM oscillators, each lagging the last, traces a traveling wave. SHM in time is a sine wave in space — the same cosine math, one dimension richer.

skeleton.text: Picture a whole row of masses on springs, each starting its swing a moment after the one before it. Photograph them all at once and you get… this — a wave.
Every point is doing pure SHM in time; strung together in space they trace a sine wave moving at v = {{waveSpeed(0.5,8)}} units/s. Same cosine heartbeat, one dimension richer.
Stretch the wavelength and watch the speed change — each point still does plain SHM, but the pattern in space breathes.

check.text: How is a traveling wave related to Simple Harmonic Motion?
options: Each point of the medium performs SHM, offset in time from its neighbors
A wave has nothing to do with SHM
Only the two endpoints of the wave move in SHM
SHM begins only after the wave has stopped
check.answer: Each point of the medium performs SHM, offset in time from its neighbors

beats.title: Deriving the spring's period
beats.goal: Derive T = 2π√(m/k) from Hooke's law and Newton's second law, one line at a time — showing the acceleration-proportional-to-displacement fingerprint of SHM and where the 2π comes from.

skeleton.title: Deriving T for a mass on a spring
notes: Hooke's restoring force equals mass times acceleration
acceleration is proportional to −x: the fingerprint of SHM
matching a = −ω²x reads off the angular frequency ω
one full cycle is 2π/ω — heavier is slower, stiffer is faster

skeleton.text: Start from what you already know: Hooke's law F = −kx set equal to Newton's ma. Let's unfold it one line at a time.
The pattern a = −(k/m)x is the fingerprint of SHM: acceleration always proportional to displacement and always pointing back. Only a cosine solves it.
The final line reads it off: T = 2π√(m/k). Check it against the very first spring — k = 20, m = 1 gives T = {{springPeriod(1,20)}} s, exactly the number it showed you.

check.text: Using T = 2π√(m/k), what is the period of a 4 kg mass on a spring with k = 16 N/m?
options: 1 s
{{springPeriod(4,16)}} s (≈ π)

beats.title: Bringing it together
beats.goal: Unify spring, pendulum, and wave under one idea — a restoring force proportional to displacement — and test the synthesis, including the classic amplitude misconception.

skeleton.text: Everything today obeyed one rule: a restoring force proportional to displacement. That single rule forces a cosine in time — the heartbeat behind them all.
Spring, pendulum, wave — same heartbeat, different clocks. Understand one and you understand them all.
One last test — drag the amplitude and watch: the period does NOT budge. Amplitude sets how big the swing is, never how fast it ticks.

check.text: What do a mass on a spring, a small-angle pendulum, and a point on a wave all share?
options: A restoring force proportional to displacement, giving cosine motion
A period that depends on the amplitude
A period that depends on the object's mass
Exactly the same period as one another
check.answer: A restoring force proportional to displacement, giving cosine motion
